chapter.title: Structure of Atom
chapter.summary:
This chapter delves into the structure of the atom, starting with the historical development of atomic theory and the discovery of subatomic particles like electrons, protons, and neutrons. Key experiments like the cathode ray tube experiment (Thomson) and Millikan's oil drop experiment are discussed, leading to the determination of the charge and mass of the electron.

The chapter then explores atomic models, contrasting Thomson's plum pudding model with Rutherford's nuclear model derived from the gold foil experiment. Rutherford's model introduces the concept of the nucleus, atomic number (Z), and mass number (A), as well as isotopes and isobars. However, the model's limitations in explaining atomic stability and spectra are highlighted.

Next, the text introduces the wave and particle nature of electromagnetic radiation, Planck's quantum theory (E=hv), the photoelectric effect, and atomic spectra. These sections pave the way for Bohr's model of the hydrogen atom, which incorporates quantized energy levels and angular momentum (mvr=nh/2π). The Rydberg formula is introduced for calculating spectral lines.

The chapter concludes with the transition to the quantum mechanical model, including de Broglie's matter-wave hypothesis (λ=h/p) and Heisenberg's uncertainty principle (ΔxΔp ≥ h/4π). Schrödinger's equation is introduced, leading to the concept of atomic orbitals described by quantum numbers (n, l, ml, ms). The shapes and energies of these orbitals are discussed, along with the rules for filling them in atoms: Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund's rule. These principles are used to determine electronic configurations, explaining the stability of half-filled and completely filled subshells. The chapter demonstrates how the understanding of atomic structure has evolved and become the basis for understanding chemical properties.

subsections.title: Historical Perspective of Atomic Theory
subsections.content_text: The concept of atoms dates back to early Indian and Greek philosophers around 400 B.C., who proposed that matter is composed of fundamental, indivisible building blocks. The term 'atom' originates from the Greek word 'a-tomio', meaning 'uncut-able' or 'non-divisible'. These early ideas remained speculative until the 19th century. In 1808, John Dalton proposed the first scientific atomic theory, which successfully explained the laws of chemical combination (conservation of mass, constant composition, and multiple proportions). However, Dalton's theory failed to explain why certain substances, like glass or ebonite, become electrically charged when rubbed with silk or fur.

subsections.title: Discovery of Sub-atomic Particles
subsections.content_text: Experimental observations at the end of the 19th and beginning of the 20th century established that atoms are not indivisible but are composed of sub-atomic particles: electrons, protons, and neutrons. A fundamental rule governing the behavior of these charged particles is that like charges repel each other and unlike charges attract each other.

subsections.title: Discovery of Electron and Cathode Ray Experiments
subsections.content_text: In 1830, Michael Faraday's work on electrolysis suggested the particulate nature of electricity. In the mid-1850s, scientists began studying electrical discharge in partially evacuated tubes called cathode ray discharge tubes. A cathode ray tube consists of a glass tube containing two thin metal pieces (electrodes) sealed within it. At very low pressures and very high voltages, an electrical discharge occurs through the gas, creating a stream of particles moving from the negative electrode (cathode) to the positive electrode (anode). These were named cathode rays or cathode ray particles.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.1(a)
diagrams.description: A basic cathode ray discharge tube showing the cathode and anode connected to a high voltage source, with a connection to a vacuum pump.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.1(b)
diagrams.description: A cathode ray discharge tube with a perforated anode and a fluorescent coating (zinc sulphide) at the end of the tube to detect the rays.

subsections.title: Properties of Cathode Rays
subsections.content_text: The results of cathode ray experiments are summarized as follows: (i) Cathode rays travel from the cathode toward the anode. (ii) The rays themselves are invisible, but their presence is detected by the glow (fluorescence) they produce when striking materials like zinc sulphide. Television picture tubes are essentially cathode ray tubes where the screen is coated with fluorescent or phosphorescent materials. (iii) In the absence of external fields, these rays travel in straight lines. (iv) In the presence of electrical or magnetic fields, they behave like negatively charged particles, which were eventually named electrons. (v) The characteristics of cathode rays (electrons) are independent of the material of the electrodes and the nature of the gas in the tube, leading to the conclusion that electrons are basic constituents of all atoms.

subsections.title: Charge to Mass Ratio of Electron
subsections.content_text: In 1897, J.J. Thomson measured the ratio of electrical charge ($e$) to the mass of the electron ($m_e$) using a cathode ray tube and applying perpendicular electrical and magnetic fields. He observed that the amount of deviation of the particles from their path depends on: (i) The magnitude of the negative charge on the particle (greater charge leads to greater interaction and deflection). (ii) The mass of the particle (lighter particles undergo greater deflection). (iii) The strength of the electrical or magnetic field (deflection increases with field strength). By balancing these fields, Thomson determined the value: $$\frac{e}{m_e} = 1.758820 \times 10^{11} \text{ C kg}^{-1}$$ where $m_e$ is the mass of the electron in kg and $e$ is the magnitude of the charge in coulombs (C).

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.2
diagrams.description: The apparatus used by J.J. Thomson to determine the charge to mass ratio of the electron, showing the cathode, anode, electric field plates, a magnet, and a fluorescent screen with points A, B, and C marking different deflection paths.

subsections.title: Charge and Mass of the Electron
subsections.content_text: R.A. Millikan (1906-14) determined the charge on the electron using the oil drop experiment. The accepted value of electrical charge is $e = -1.602176 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$. By combining this with Thomson's $e/m_e$ ratio, the mass of the electron ($m_e$) was calculated as: $$m_e = \frac{e}{e/m_e} = \frac{1.602176 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}{1.758820 \times 10^{11} \text{ C kg}^{-1}} = 9.1094 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$$

subsections.title: Discovery of Protons and Neutrons
subsections.content_text: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube led to the discovery of canal rays carrying positively charged particles. Unlike cathode rays, the mass of these positively charged particles depends upon the nature of the gas present in the cathode ray tube; they are simply the positively charged gaseous ions. The charge to mass ratio of these particles depends on the gas from which they originate. Some of the positively charged particles carry a multiple of the fundamental unit of electrical charge. Furthermore, the behaviour of these particles in the magnetic or electrical field is opposite to that observed for electrons or cathode rays. The smallest and lightest positive ion was obtained from hydrogen and was called a proton, which was characterized in 1919.

subsections.title: Discovery of Neutrons
subsections.content_text: A need was felt for the presence of an electrically neutral particle as one of the constituents of the atom. These particles were discovered by James Chadwick in 1932 by bombarding a thin sheet of beryllium with $\alpha$-particles. When electrically neutral particles having a mass slightly greater than that of protons were emitted, he named these particles as neutrons. The properties of these fundamental particles (electrons, protons, and neutrons) are summarized in Table 2.1.

subsections.title: Millikan’s Oil Drop Method
subsections.content_text: In this method, oil droplets in the form of mist, produced by an atomizer, were allowed to enter through a tiny hole in the upper plate of an electrical condenser. The downward motion of these droplets was viewed through a telescope equipped with a micrometer eyepiece. By measuring the rate of fall of these droplets, Millikan was able to measure the mass of oil droplets. The air inside the chamber was ionized by passing a beam of X-rays through it. The electrical charge on these oil droplets was acquired by collisions with gaseous ions. The fall of these charged oil droplets can be retarded, accelerated, or made stationary depending upon the charge on the droplets and the polarity and strength of the voltage applied to the plate. By carefully measuring the effects of electrical field strength on the motion of oil droplets, Millikan concluded that the magnitude of electrical charge, $q$, on the droplets is always an integral multiple of the electrical charge, $e$, that is, $q = ne$, where $n = 1, 2, 3...$.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.3
diagrams.description: The Millikan oil drop apparatus showing an atomizer producing oil droplets, a telescope for observation, a chamber with charged plates (positive and negative), and an X-ray source for ionizing the air. The diagram illustrates the forces acting on an oil drop: gravitational, electrostatic, and viscous drag.

subsections.title: Challenges in Atomic Theory
subsections.content_text: After the discovery of sub-atomic particles, the major problems facing scientists were: (i) to account for the stability of the atom, (ii) to compare the behaviour of elements in terms of both physical and chemical properties, (iii) to explain the formation of different kinds of molecules by the combination of different atoms, and (iv) to understand the origin and nature of the characteristics of electromagnetic radiation absorbed or emitted by atoms. Different atomic models were proposed to explain the distributions of these charged particles in an atom.

subsections.title: X-rays and Radioactivity
subsections.content_text: In the late 19th century, Wilhalm Röentgen showed that when electrons strike a material in cathode ray tubes, they produce X-rays, which cause fluorescence and are not deflected by electric or magnetic fields. Henri Becquerel observed that certain elements emit radiation spontaneously, a phenomenon named radioactivity. Radioactive elements emit three types of rays: $\alpha$-rays (high energy particles with $+2$ charge and $4$ u mass, identified as helium nuclei), $\beta$-rays (negatively charged particles similar to electrons), and $\gamma$-rays (high energy neutral radiations). The penetrating power follows the order: $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$.

subsections.title: Thomson Model of Atom
subsections.content_text: J. J. Thomson, in 1898, proposed that an atom possesses a spherical shape (radius approximately $10^{-10}$ m) in which the positive charge is uniformly distributed. The electrons are embedded into it in such a manner as to give the most stable electrostatic arrangement. This model is often called the plum pudding, raisin pudding, or watermelon model. It visualizes the positive charge as the pudding or watermelon flesh, with electrons as the plums or seeds. While it explained the overall neutrality of the atom, it was inconsistent with later experimental results.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.4
diagrams.description: Thomson model of atom depicted as a positive sphere with electrons embedded within it, resembling seeds in a watermelon.

subsections.title: Rutherford’s Nuclear Model of Atom
subsections.content_text: Ernest Rutherford and his students (Hans Geiger and Ernest Marsden) bombarded a very thin gold foil (thickness $\sim 100$ nm) with a stream of high energy $\alpha$-particles from a radioactive source. A circular fluorescent zinc sulphide screen was placed around the foil to detect the particles; whenever an $\alpha$-particle struck the screen, a tiny flash of light was produced.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.5
diagrams.description: Schematic view of Rutherford’s scattering experiment. Part A shows the setup with a source of alpha particles, a lead plate, gold foil, and a photographic plate. Part B shows a molecular view where most alpha particles pass through the gold atoms undeflected, while a few are deflected at various angles.

subsections.title: Observations and Conclusions of Rutherford's Experiment
subsections.content_text: Rutherford observed that: (i) most $\alpha$-particles passed through the gold foil undeflected, (ii) a small fraction were deflected by small angles, and (iii) very few (about $1$ in $20,000$) bounced back ($180^{\circ}$ deflection). From these, he concluded: (i) Most of the space in the atom is empty. (ii) The positive charge and most of the mass are concentrated in a very small volume called the nucleus. (iii) The volume occupied by the nucleus is negligibly small compared to the total volume of the atom. The radius of the atom is about $10^{-10}$ m, while that of the nucleus is $10^{-15}$ m.

subsections.title: Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
subsections.content_text: Based on the observations and conclusions of the $\alpha$-particle scattering experiment, Rutherford proposed the nuclear model of the atom. To illustrate the scale of the nucleus, he noted that if a cricket ball represents a nucleus, then the radius of the atom would be about 5 km. The key features of this model are: (i) The positive charge and most of the mass of the atom are densely concentrated in an extremely small region called the nucleus. (ii) The nucleus is surrounded by electrons that move around it at very high speeds in circular paths called orbits. This model resembles the solar system, where the nucleus plays the role of the sun and the electrons act as revolving planets. (iii) Electrons and the nucleus are held together by electrostatic forces of attraction.

subsections.title: Atomic Number and Mass Number
subsections.content_text: The positive charge of the nucleus is due to the presence of protons. The number of protons in the nucleus is defined as the atomic number ($Z$). For example, the number of protons in a hydrogen nucleus is 1 and in a sodium atom is 11, so their atomic numbers are 1 and 11 respectively. To maintain electrical neutrality, the number of electrons in a neutral atom is equal to the number of protons. Thus: Atomic number ($Z$) = number of protons in the nucleus of an atom = number of electrons in a neutral atom (Equation 2.3). While the positive charge is due to protons, the mass of the nucleus is due to both protons and neutrons, which are collectively known as nucleons. The total number of nucleons is termed the mass number ($A$). Thus: mass number ($A$) = number of protons ($Z$) + number of neutrons ($n$) (Equation 2.4).

subsections.title: Isobars and Isotopes
subsections.content_text: The composition of any atom can be represented using the symbol ${}_{Z}^{A}X$, where $X$ is the element symbol, $A$ is the mass number (superscript), and $Z$ is the atomic number (subscript). Isobars are atoms with the same mass number but different atomic numbers, such as ${}_{6}^{14}C$ and ${}_{7}^{14}N$. Isotopes are atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers. This difference is due to the number of neutrons in the nucleus. For example, hydrogen has three isotopes: protium (${}_{1}^{1}H$, 99.985%), deuterium (${}_{1}^{2}D$ or $D$, 0.015%), and tritium (${}_{1}^{3}T$ or $T$, trace amounts). Other examples include carbon (${}_{6}^{12}C, {}_{6}^{13}C, {}_{6}^{14}C$) and chlorine (${}_{17}^{35}Cl, {}_{17}^{37}Cl$). Chemical properties are determined by the number of electrons (and thus protons), so all isotopes of a given element show the same chemical behavior.

subsections.title: Worked Examples on Atomic Composition
subsections.content_text: This subsection provides practical applications for calculating subatomic particles in neutral atoms and ions. Note: Before using the notation ${}_{Z}^{A}X$, determine if the species is neutral, a cation, or an anion. For neutral atoms, the number of protons equals the number of electrons. For ions, the number of protons is larger (cation) or smaller (anion) than the number of electrons. The number of neutrons is always $A - Z$.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.1
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the number of protons, neutrons and electrons in ${}_{35}^{80}Br$.
worked_examples.solution: In this case, ${}_{35}^{80}Br$, $Z = 35$ and $A = 80$. Since the species is neutral, Number of protons = number of electrons = $Z = 35$. Number of neutrons = $A - Z = 80 - 35 = 45$.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.2
worked_examples.problem: The number of electrons, protons and neutrons in a species are equal to 18, 16 and 16 respectively. Assign the proper symbol to the species.
worked_examples.solution: The atomic number is equal to the number of protons = 16, which identifies the element as sulphur (S). Atomic mass number = number of protons + number of neutrons = $16 + 16 = 32$. Since the number of electrons (18) is greater than the number of protons (16), the species is an anion with a charge of $18 - 16 = 2$. The symbol is ${}_{16}^{32}S^{2-}$.

subsections.title: Drawbacks of Rutherford Model
subsections.content_text: Rutherford's model faces significant challenges regarding stability and electronic structure. 1. Stability: According to classical mechanics (the science of macroscopic motion) and Maxwell's electromagnetic theory, an accelerating charged particle (like an electron in orbit) must emit electromagnetic radiation. This loss of energy would cause the electron to spiral into the nucleus in approximately $10^{-8}$ s, making the atom unstable. However, atoms are stable. 2. Electronic Structure: The model does not explain the distribution of electrons around the nucleus or their specific energies. Additionally, if electrons were stationary, electrostatic attraction would pull them into the nucleus, forming a miniature Thomson model. The model's comparison to the solar system is mathematically similar because the Coulomb force ($k \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$) follows the same inverse-square law as the gravitational force ($G \frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$).

sections.title: DEVELOPMENTS LEADING TO THE BOHR’S MODEL OF ATOM

subsections.title: Key Developments for Bohr's Model
subsections.content_text: Neils Bohr improved Rutherford's model by incorporating new findings regarding the interaction of radiation with matter. Two major developments were: (i) The dual character of electromagnetic radiation, which possesses both wave-like and particle-like properties. (ii) Experimental results regarding atomic spectra, which suggested quantized energy levels.

subsections.title: Wave Nature of Electromagnetic Radiation
subsections.content_text: In the mid-19th century, physicists studied thermal radiation emitted by heated objects. James Clerk Maxwell (1870) provided a comprehensive explanation of the interaction between charged bodies and electric/magnetic fields. He proposed that when an electrically charged particle accelerates, alternating electrical and magnetic fields are produced and transmitted as electromagnetic waves. While Newton originally thought light consisted of particles (corpuscules), the 19th century established its wave nature, later confirmed experimentally by Heinrich Hertz.

subsections.title: Characteristics of Electromagnetic Waves
subsections.content_text: Maxwell revealed that light waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic characters. Key properties include: (i) The oscillating electric and magnetic fields produced by oscillating charged particles are perpendicular to each other and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave (Fig. 2.6). These components share the same wavelength, frequency, speed, and amplitude. particles are perpendicular to each other and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave (Fig. 2.6). These components share the same wavelength, frequency, speed, and amplitude. (ii) Unlike sound waves or waves produced in water, electromagnetic waves do not require a medium and can move in a vacuum.

diagrams.label: Fig 2.6
diagrams.description: A 3D representation of an electromagnetic wave showing the electric field component (vibrating in the vertical z-plane) and the magnetic field component (vibrating in the horizontal y-plane). Both fields are perpendicular to each other and to the x-axis, which represents the direction of propagation.

subsections.title: The Electromagnetic Spectrum
subsections.content_text: (iii) It is now well established that there are many types of electromagnetic radiations, which differ from one another in wavelength (or frequency). These constitute what is called the electromagnetic spectrum (Fig. 2.7). Different regions of the spectrum are identified by different names. Some examples are: the radio frequency region around $10^6$ Hz, used for broadcasting; the microwave region around $10^{10}$ Hz used for radar; the infrared region around $10^{13}$ Hz used for heating; and the ultraviolet region around $10^{16}$ Hz, a component of sun’s radiation. The small portion around $10^{15}$ Hz is what is ordinarily called visible light. It is only this part which our eyes can see (or detect). Special instruments are required to detect non-visible radiation.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.7
diagrams.description: (a) The spectrum of electromagnetic radiation showing various regions including gamma rays, X-rays, UV, IR, Microwaves (FM, AM, Radio waves), and Long radio waves with their corresponding frequencies (v in Hz) and wavelengths (lambda in m). (b) An expanded view of the visible spectrum ranging from approximately 400 nm (violet) to 750 nm (red).

subsections.title: Units and Properties of Electromagnetic Radiation
subsections.content_text: (iv) Different kinds of units are used to represent electromagnetic radiation. These radiations are characterised by the properties, namely, frequency ($\nu$) and wavelength ($\lambda$). The SI unit for frequency ($\nu$) is hertz (Hz, $\text{s}^{-1}$), named after Heinrich Hertz. It is defined as the number of waves that pass a given point in one second. Wavelength should have the units of length, and the SI unit of length is the meter (m). Since electromagnetic radiation consists of different kinds of waves of much smaller wavelengths, smaller units are often used.

subsections.title: Relationship between Speed, Frequency, and Wavelength
subsections.content_text: In vacuum, all types of electromagnetic radiations, regardless of wavelength, travel at the same speed, i.e., $3.0 \times 10^8$ $\text{m s}^{-1}$ ($2.997925 \times 10^8$ $\text{m s}^{-1}$, to be precise). This is called the speed of light and is given the symbol 'c'. The frequency ($\nu$), wavelength ($\lambda$), and velocity of light (c) are related by the equation: $$c = \nu \lambda$$

subsections.title: Wavenumber
subsections.content_text: The other commonly used quantity, especially in spectroscopy, is the wavenumber ($\bar{\nu}$). It is defined as the number of wavelengths per unit length. Its units are reciprocal of the wavelength unit, i.e., $\text{m}^{-1}$. However, the commonly used unit is $\text{cm}^{-1}$ (not an SI unit). It is mathematically expressed as: $$\bar{\nu} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

subsections.title: Worked Example: Radio Wave Wavelength
subsections.content_text: Calculation of wavelength for a given broadcast frequency.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.3
worked_examples.problem: The Vividh Bharati station of All India Radio, Delhi, broadcasts on a frequency of 1,368 kHz (kilo hertz). Calculate the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation emitted by transmitter. Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong to?
worked_examples.solution:
The wavelength, $\lambda$, is equal to $c/
u$, where c is the speed of electromagnetic radiation in vacuum and $
u$ is the frequency. Substituting the given values, we have: $\lambda =  rac{c}{
u} =  rac{3.00 	imes 10^8 	ext{ m s}^{-1}}{1368 	ext{ kHz}} =  rac{3.00 	imes 10^8 	ext{ m s}^{-1}}{1368 	imes 10^3 	ext{ s}^{-1}} = 219.3 	ext{ m}$. This is a characteristic radiowave wavelength.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Visible Spectrum Frequencies
subsections.content_text: Converting the wavelength range of visible light into frequency units.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.4
worked_examples.problem: The wavelength range of the visible spectrum extends from violet (400 nm) to red (750 nm). Express these wavelengths in frequencies (Hz). ($1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$)
worked_examples.solution: Using equation 2.5, frequency of violet light: $\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{400 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 7.50 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$. Frequency of red light: $\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{750 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 4.00 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$. The range of visible spectrum is from $4.0 \times 10^{14}$ to $7.5 \times 10^{14}$ Hz in terms of frequency units.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Wavenumber and Frequency
subsections.content_text: Calculating wavenumber and frequency from a specific wavelength in Angstroms.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.5
worked_examples.problem: Calculate (a) wavenumber and (b) frequency of yellow radiation having wavelength 5800 Å.

sections.title: Particle Nature of Electromagnetic Radiation: Planck’s Quantum Theory

subsections.title: Limitations of Classical Wave Theory
subsections.content_text: Experimental phenomena such as diffraction (the bending of waves around an obstacle) and interference (the combination of waves to produce a resultant disturbance) are explained by the wave nature of electromagnetic radiation. However, classical 19th-century physics failed to explain: (i) the nature of emission of radiation from hot bodies (black-body radiation), (ii) the ejection of electrons from metal surfaces when radiation strikes it (photoelectric effect), (iii) the variation of heat capacity of solids as a function of temperature, and (iv) line spectra of atoms, specifically hydrogen. These phenomena indicate that systems can take energy only in discrete amounts, meaning all possible energies cannot be taken up or radiated.

subsections.title: Black-body Radiation
subsections.content_text: It is noteworthy that the first concrete explanation for the phenomenon of the black body radiation was given by Max Planck in 1900. Hot objects emit electromagnetic radiations over a wide range of wavelengths. At high temperatures, an appreciable proportion of radiation is in the visible region of the spectrum. As the temperature is raised, a higher proportion of short wavelength (blue light) is generated. For example, when an iron rod is heated in a furnace, it first turns to dull red and then progressively becomes more and more red as the temperature increases. As this is heated further, the radiation emitted becomes white and then becomes blue as the temperature becomes very high. This means that red radiation is most intense at a particular temperature and the blue radiation is more intense at another temperature. This means intensities of radiations of different wavelengths emitted by hot body depend upon its temperature. By late 1850's it was known that objects made of different material and kept at different temperatures emit different amount of radiation. Also, when the surface of an object is irradiated with light (electromagnetic radiation), a part of radiant energy is generally reflected as such, a part is absorbed and a part of it is transmitted. The reason for incomplete absorption is that ordinary objects are as a rule imperfect absorbers of radiation. An ideal body, which emits and absorbs radiations of all frequencies uniformly, is called a black body and the radiation emitted by such a body is called black body radiation. In practice, no such body exists. Carbon black approximates fairly closely to black body. A good physical approximation to a black body is a cavity with a tiny hole, which has no other opening. Any ray entering the hole will be reflected by the cavity walls and will be eventually absorbed by the walls. A black body is also a perfect radiator of radiant energy. Furthermore, a black body is in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings. It radiates same amount of energy per unit area as it absorbs from its surrounding in any given time. The amount of light emitted (intensity of radiation) from a black body and its spectral distribution depends only on its temperature. At a given temperature, intensity of radiation emitted increases with the increase of wavelength, reaches a maximum value at a given wavelength and then starts decreasing with further increase of wavelength, as shown in Fig. 2.8. Also, as the temperature increases, maxima of the curve shifts to short wavelength. Several attempts were made to predict the intensity of radiation as a function of wavelength. But the results of the above experiment could not be explained satisfactorily on the basis of the wave theory of light. Max Planck arrived at a satisfactory relationship by making an assumption that absorption and emission of radiation arises from oscillators, i.e., atoms in the wall of black body. Their frequency of oscillation is changed by interaction with oscillators of electromagnetic radiation. Planck assumed that radiation could be sub-divided into discrete chunks of energy. He suggested that atoms and molecules could emit or absorb energy only in discrete quantities and not in a continuous manner. He gave the name quantum to the smallest quantity of energy that can be emitted or absorbed in the form of electromagnetic radiation.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.8
diagrams.description: A graph of intensity versus wavelength for black-body radiation at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ ($T_2 > T_1$). It shows that as temperature increases, the peak intensity increases and the corresponding wavelength of maximum emission shifts to the left (shorter wavelengths).

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.8(a)
diagrams.description: A schematic of a black body represented as a hollow sphere with a small hole. An incident ray entering the hole undergoes multiple internal reflections, illustrating how the cavity acts as a perfect absorber.

subsections.title: Definition of Quantum and Energy Equation
subsections.content_text: Planck gave the name 'quantum' to the smallest quantity of energy that can be emitted or absorbed in the form of electromagnetic radiation. The energy ($E$) of a quantum of radiation is proportional to its frequency ($\nu$) and is expressed by the equation $E = h\nu$. The proportionality constant, '$h$', is known as Planck’s constant and has the value $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J s. With this theory, Planck was able to explain the distribution of intensity in the radiation from black body as a function of frequency or wavelength at different temperatures.

subsections.title: Quantisation Analogy
subsections.content_text:
Quantisation has been compared to standing on a staircase. A person can stand on any step of a staircase, but it is not possible for him/her to stand in between the two steps. The energy can take any one of the values from the following set: $E = 0, h
u, 2h
u, 3h
u \dots nh
u \dots$, but cannot take on any values between them.

subsections.title: Worked Example 2.6
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates how to calculate the energy of a mole of photons given the frequency of radiation. Using Planck's equation $E = h\nu$, the energy of a single photon is first determined. Then, multiplying by Avogadro's number ($N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$) gives the total energy per mole.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.6
worked_examples.problem: Calculate energy of one mole of photons of radiation whose frequency is $5 \times 10^{14}$ Hz.
worked_examples.solution: Energy ($E$) of one photon is given by the expression $E = h\nu$. Given $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J s and $\nu = 5 \times 10^{14}$ s$^{-1}$. $E = (6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (5 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}) = 3.313 \times 10^{-19}$ J. Energy of one mole of photons = $(3.313 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}) \times (6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}) = 199.51$ kJ mol$^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Worked Example 2.7
subsections.content_text: This example shows how to find the number of photons emitted per second by a light source of known power and wavelength. Power is energy per unit time ($100$ W = $100$ J/s). The energy of one photon is calculated using $E = hc/\lambda$. Dividing the total power by the energy of one photon yields the number of photons emitted per second.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.7
worked_examples.problem: A 100 watt bulb emits monochromatic light of wavelength 400 nm. Calculate the number of photons emitted per second by the bulb.
worked_examples.solution: Power of the bulb = 100 watt = 100 J s$^{-1}$. Energy of one photon $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s} \times 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{400 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 4.969 \times 10^{-19}$ J. Number of photons emitted = $\frac{100 \text{ J s}^{-1}}{4.969 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}} = 2.012 \times 10^{20}$ s$^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Photoelectric Effect - Experimental Observations
subsections.content_text:
In 1887, H. Hertz performed an experiment in which electrons (or electric current) were ejected when certain metals (for example potassium, rubidium, caesium etc.) were exposed to a beam of light. The phenomenon is called Photoelectric effect. The results observed were: (i) The electrons are ejected from the metal surface as soon as the beam of light strikes the surface, i.e., there is no time lag. (ii) The number of electrons ejected is proportional to the intensity or brightness of light. (iii) For each metal, there is a characteristic minimum frequency, $
u_0$ (threshold frequency) below which photoelectric effect is not observed. At a frequency $
u > 
u_0$, the ejected electrons come out with certain kinetic energy. The kinetic energies of these electrons increase with the increase of frequency of the light used.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.9
diagrams.description: Equipment for studying the photoelectric effect. Light of a particular frequency strikes a clean metal surface inside a vacuum chamber. Electrons are ejected from the metal and are counted by a detector that measures their kinetic energy. The setup includes an ammeter to measure current and a battery.

subsections.title: Einstein's Explanation and the Photon Concept
subsections.content_text: Classical physics could not explain the photoelectric effect because it predicted that the energy of the beam depends on brightness (intensity), implying that kinetic energy should increase with brightness. Albert Einstein (1879-1955) resolved this in 1905 by applying Planck’s quantum theory. He proposed that light consists of particles called photons. When a photon of sufficient energy strikes an electron, it transfers its energy instantaneously to the electron during the collision and the electron is ejected without any time lag. Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for his explanation of the photoelectric effect.

subsections.title: Mathematical Formulation and Work Function
subsections.content_text:
Since the striking photon has energy equal to $h
u$ and the minimum energy required to eject the electron is $h
u_0$ (also called work function, $W_0$), then the difference in energy ($h
u - h
u_0$) is transferred as the kinetic energy of the photoelectron. Following the conservation of energy principle, the kinetic energy of the ejected electron is given by the equation: $h
u = h
u_0 +  rac{1}{2}m_e v^2$, where $m_e$ is the mass of the electron and $v$ is its velocity. A more intense beam of light consists of larger number of photons, consequently the number of electrons ejected is also larger. Table 2.2 lists work function values for various metals.

tables.label: Table 2.2
tables.caption: Values of Work Function ($W_0$) for a Few Metals

subsections.title: Worked Example 2.8
subsections.content_text: This example applies Einstein's photoelectric equation to sodium. Given the wavelength of incident light and the kinetic energy of ejected electrons per mole, the work function ($W_0$) is calculated as the difference between photon energy and kinetic energy. The threshold wavelength ($\lambda_0$) is then found using $\lambda_0 = hc/W_0$.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.8
worked_examples.problem: When electromagnetic radiation of wavelength 300 nm falls on the surface of sodium, electrons are emitted with a kinetic energy of $1.68 \times 10^5$ J mol$^{-1}$. What is the minimum energy needed to remove an electron from sodium? What is the maximum wavelength that will cause a photoelectron to be emitted?
worked_examples.solution: Energy ($E$) of a 300 nm photon is $E = hc/\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s} \times 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{300 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 6.626 \times 10^{-19}$ J. Energy of one mole of photons = $6.626 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1} = 3.99 \times 10^5$ J mol$^{-1}$. The minimum energy needed to remove one mole of electrons from sodium = $(3.99 - 1.68) \times 10^5$ J mol$^{-1} = 2.31 \times 10^5$ J mol$^{-1}$. The minimum energy for one electron = $\frac{2.31 \times 10^5 \text{ J mol}^{-1}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ electrons mol}^{-1}} = 3.84 \times 10^{-19}$ J. This corresponds to the wavelength $\lambda = hc/E = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s} \times 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{3.84 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}} = 517$ nm (green light).

subsections.title: Worked Example 2.9
subsections.content_text:
This example calculates the kinetic energy of an ejected electron given the threshold frequency ($
u_0$) and the frequency of incident radiation ($
u$). The formula $K.E. = h(
u - 
u_0)$ is used, which is a direct application of Einstein's photoelectric equation.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.9
worked_examples.problem: The threshold frequency $\nu_0$ for a metal is $7.0 \times 10^{14}$ s$^{-1}$. Calculate the kinetic energy of an electron emitted when radiation of frequency $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{15}$ s$^{-1}$ hits the metal.
worked_examples.solution: According to Einstein's equation, Kinetic energy = $\frac{1}{2}m_e v^2 = h(\nu - \nu_0)$. $= (6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1} - 7.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1})$ $= (6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1})$ $= 1.988 \times 10^{-19}$ J.

subsections.title: Dual Behaviour of Electromagnetic Radiation
subsections.content_text: The particle nature of light posed a dilemma for scientists. On the one hand, it could explain the black body radiation and photoelectric effect satisfactorily but on the other hand, it was not consistent with the known wave behaviour of light which could account for the phenomena of interference and diffraction. The only way to resolve the dilemma was to accept the idea that light possesses both particle and wave-like properties, i.e., light has dual behaviour. Depending on the experiment, we find that light behaves either as a wave or as a stream of particles. Whenever radiation interacts with matter, it displays particle like properties in contrast to the wavelike properties (interference and diffraction), which it exhibits when it propagates. This concept was totally alien to the way the scientists thought about matter and radiation and it took them a long time to become convinced of its validity. It turns out, as you shall see later, that some microscopic particles like electrons also exhibit this wave-particle duality.

sections.title: Evidence for the Quantized Electronic Energy Levels: Atomic Spectra

subsections.title: Refraction and the Continuous Spectrum
subsections.content_text: The speed of light depends upon the nature of the medium through which it passes. When a beam of light passes from one medium to another, it is deviated or refracted from its original path. It is observed that when a ray of white light is passed through a prism, the wave with shorter wavelength bends more than the one with a longer wavelength. Since ordinary white light consists of waves with all the wavelengths in the visible range, a ray of white light is spread out into a series of coloured bands called a spectrum. The light of red colour, which has the longest wavelength, is deviated the least, while the violet light, which has the shortest wavelength, is deviated the most. The spectrum of white light ranges from violet at $7.50 \times 10^{14}$ Hz to red at $4 \times 10^{14}$ Hz. Such a spectrum is called a continuous spectrum because violet merges into blue, blue into green, and so on, without gaps. A similar spectrum is produced when a rainbow forms in the sky. Visible light is just a small portion of the electromagnetic radiation.

subsections.title: Emission and Absorption Spectra
subsections.content_text: When electromagnetic radiation interacts with matter, atoms and molecules may absorb energy and reach a higher, unstable energy state. For returning to their normal, more stable, lower energy states, the atoms and molecules emit radiations in various regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. The spectrum of radiation emitted by a substance that has absorbed energy is called an emission spectrum. Atoms, molecules, or ions that have absorbed radiation are said to be "excited". To produce an emission spectrum, energy is supplied to a sample by heating it or irradiating it, and the wavelength (or frequency) of the radiation emitted as the sample gives up the absorbed energy is recorded. An absorption spectrum is like the photographic negative of an emission spectrum. A continuum of radiation is passed through a sample which absorbs radiation of certain wavelengths. The missing wavelengths, which correspond to the radiation absorbed by the matter, leave dark spaces in the bright continuous spectrum.

subsections.title: Quantization
subsections.content_text: The restriction of any property to discrete values is called quantization. This concept is fundamental to understanding why atomic spectra consist of discrete lines rather than a continuous spread of wavelengths.

subsections.title: Spectroscopy and Atomic Line Spectra
subsections.content_text: The study of emission or absorption spectra is referred to as spectroscopy. Unlike the continuous spectrum of visible light, the emission spectra of atoms in the gas phase do not show a continuous spread of wavelength from red to violet; rather, they emit light only at specific wavelengths with dark spaces between them. Such spectra are called line spectra or atomic spectra because the emitted radiation is identified by the appearance of bright lines in the spectra (Fig. 2.10). Line emission spectra are of great interest in the study of electronic structure. Each element has a unique line emission spectrum, which can be used in chemical analysis to identify unknown atoms in the same way as fingerprints are used to identify people. German chemist Robert Bunsen (1811-1899) was one of the first investigators to use line spectra to identify elements. Elements like rubidium (Rb), caesium (Cs), thallium (Tl), indium (In), gallium (Ga), and scandium (Sc) were discovered when their minerals were analysed by spectroscopic methods. The element helium (He) was discovered in the sun by spectroscopic method.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.10
diagrams.description: (a) Atomic emission: Light emitted by a sample of excited hydrogen atoms (or any other element) is passed through a prism and separated into certain discrete wavelengths recorded as a line spectrum. (b) Atomic absorption: White light is passed through unexcited atomic hydrogen and then through a slit and prism; the transmitted light is lacking in intensity at the same wavelengths as are emitted in (a), recorded as an absorption spectrum which is the photographic negative of the emission spectrum.

subsections.title: The Line Spectrum of Hydrogen and the Balmer Series
subsections.content_text: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the $H_2$ molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produced emit electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies. The hydrogen spectrum consists of several series of lines named after their discoverers. Balmer showed in 1885, on the basis of experimental observations, that if spectral lines are expressed in terms of wavenumber ($\bar{\nu}$), then the visible lines of the hydrogen spectrum obey the following formula: $\bar{\nu} = 109,677 \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{n^2} \right) \text{ cm}^{-1}$, where $n$ is an integer equal to or greater than 3 (i.e., $n = 3, 4, 5, \dots$). The series of lines described by this formula are called the Balmer series. These are the only lines in the hydrogen spectrum which appear in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

subsections.title: The Rydberg Formula and Spectral Series of Hydrogen
subsections.content_text: The Swedish spectroscopist Johannes Rydberg noted that all series of lines in the hydrogen spectrum could be described by the following expression: $\bar{\nu} = 109,677 \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right) \text{ cm}^{-1}$, where $n_1 = 1, 2, \dots$ and $n_2 = n_1 + 1, n_1 + 2, \dots$. The value $109,677 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ is called the Rydberg constant for hydrogen. The first five series of lines that correspond to $n_1 = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ are known as Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett, and Pfund series, respectively (Table 2.3). Fig. 2.11 shows the Lyman, Balmer, and Paschen series of transitions for the hydrogen atom. Of all the elements, the hydrogen atom has the simplest line spectrum, which becomes more complex for heavier atoms. However, all line spectra share common features: (i) the line spectrum of an element is unique and (ii) there is regularity in the line spectrum of each element. These similarities provide the key to understanding the electronic structure of atoms.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.11
diagrams.description: Energy level diagram showing transitions of the electron in the hydrogen atom. It depicts the Lyman series (transitions to $n=1$ in the ultraviolet region), Balmer series (transitions to $n=2$ in the visible region with red, green, blue, and violet lines), and Paschen series (transitions to $n=3$ in the infrared region).

tables.label: Table 2.3
tables.caption: The Spectral Lines for Atomic Hydrogen

subsections.title: Introduction to Bohr's Model
subsections.content_text: Neils Bohr (1913) was the first to explain quantitatively the general features of the structure of the hydrogen atom and its spectrum. He used Planck’s concept of quantisation of energy. Though the theory is not modern quantum mechanics, it can still be used to rationalize many points in atomic structure and spectra. Bohr’s model for the hydrogen atom is based on several key postulates.

subsections.title: Postulate 1: Orbits and Stationary States
subsections.content_text: The electron in the hydrogen atom can move around the nucleus in a circular path of fixed radius and energy. These paths are called orbits, stationary states, or allowed energy states. These orbits are arranged concentrically around the nucleus.

subsections.title: Postulate 2: Energy Transitions in Orbits
subsections.content_text: The energy of an electron in the orbit does not change with time. However, the electron will move from a lower stationary state to a higher stationary state when a required amount of energy is absorbed by the electron, or energy is emitted when the electron moves from a higher stationary state to a lower stationary state. The energy change does not take place in a continuous manner.

subsections.title: Angular Momentum of an Electron
subsections.content_text: Just as linear momentum is the product of mass ($m$) and linear velocity ($v$), angular momentum is the product of moment of inertia ($I$) and angular velocity ($\omega$). For an electron of mass $m_e$ moving in a circular path of radius $r$ around the nucleus, angular momentum $L = I \times \omega$. Since $I = m_e r^2$ and $\omega = v/r$, the angular momentum is given by: $L = m_e r^2 \times (v/r) = m_e v r$.

subsections.title: Postulate 3: Bohr’s Frequency Rule
subsections.content_text: The frequency ($\nu$) of radiation absorbed or emitted when a transition occurs between two stationary states that differ in energy by $\Delta E$ is given by: $\nu = \frac{\Delta E}{h} = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{h}$, where $E_1$ and $E_2$ are the energies of the lower and higher allowed energy states, respectively. This expression is commonly known as Bohr’s frequency rule.

subsections.title: Postulate 4: Quantization of Angular Momentum
subsections.content_text: The angular momentum of an electron is quantised. In a given stationary state, it can be expressed as: $m_e v r = n \frac{h}{2\pi}$, where $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$. This means that an electron can move only in those orbits for which its angular momentum is an integral multiple of $h/2\pi$. Where $m_e$ is the mass of electron, $v$ is the velocity and $r$ is the radius of the orbit in which electron is moving. Thus an electron can move only in those orbits for which its angular momentum is integral multiple of $h/2\pi$. That means angular momentum is quantised. Radiation is emitted or absorbed only when transition of electron takes place from one quantised value of angular momentum to another. Therefore, Maxwell’s electromagnetic theory does not apply here that is why only certain fixed orbits are allowed. The details regarding the derivation of energies of the stationary states used by Bohr, are quite complicated and will be discussed in higher classes.

subsections.title: Biography: Niels Bohr
subsections.content_text: Niels Bohr (1885–1962) was a Danish physicist who received his Ph.D. from the University of Copenhagen in 1911. He spent time with J.J. Thomson and Ernest Rutherford in England. In 1913, he returned to Copenhagen and was later named Director of the Institute of Theoretical Physics in 1920. Bohr worked on the peaceful uses of atomic energy and received the first Atoms for Peace award in 1957. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1922.

diagrams.label: Niels Bohr Portrait
diagrams.description: A portrait of Niels Bohr, the Danish physicist known for his work on atomic structure and quantum theory.

subsections.title: Principal Quantum Numbers and Bohr Orbits
subsections.content_text: According to Bohr’s theory for hydrogen atom, the stationary states for electron are numbered $n = 1, 2, 3 \dots$. These integral numbers are known as Principal quantum numbers. The radii of the stationary states are expressed as: $r_n = n^2 a_0$ (Eq. 2.12), where $a_0 = 52.9$ pm. The radius of the first stationary state ($n=1$), called the Bohr orbit, is $52.9$ pm. As $n$ increases, the value of $r$ increases, meaning the electron is present further away from the nucleus.

subsections.title: Energy of Stationary States in Hydrogen
subsections.content_text: The energy of a stationary state is given by the expression: $E_n = -R_H (\frac{1}{n^2})$ for $n = 1, 2, 3 \dots$ (Eq. 2.13), where $R_H$ is the Rydberg constant ($2.18 \times 10^{-18}$ J). The energy of the ground state ($n=1$) is $E_1 = -2.18 \times 10^{-18} (\frac{1}{1^2}) = -2.18 \times 10^{-18}$ J. For $n=2$, the energy is $E_2 = -2.18 \times 10^{-18} J (\frac{1}{2^2}) = -0.545 \times 10^{-18}$ J. An energy level diagram (Fig. 2.11) can be used to depict these energies.

subsections.title: Significance of Negative Electronic Energy
subsections.content_text: The negative sign in the energy expression (Eq. 2.13) indicates that the energy of the electron in the atom is lower than the energy of a free electron at rest. A free electron at rest, infinitely far from the nucleus, is assigned an energy of zero ($E_\infty = 0$). As the electron gets closer to the nucleus ($n$ decreases), $E_n$ becomes more negative, reaching its most negative value at $n=1$ (the ground state), which is the most stable orbit.

subsections.title: Bohr's Theory for Hydrogen-like Species
subsections.content_text: Bohr’s theory applies to ions with only one electron, such as $He^+$, $Li^{2+}$, and $Be^{3+}$. The energies of stationary states for these hydrogen-like species are: $E_n = -2.18 \times 10^{-18} (\frac{Z^2}{n^2})$ J (Eq. 2.14). The radii are given by: $r_n = \frac{52.9(n^2)}{Z}$ pm (Eq. 2.15), where $Z$ is the atomic number. As $Z$ increases, energy becomes more negative and the radius decreases, indicating the electron is more tightly bound to the nucleus.

subsections.title: Electron Velocity in Orbits
subsections.content_text: Qualitatively, the magnitude of the velocity of an electron increases with the increase of positive charge on the nucleus ($Z$) and decreases with the increase of the principal quantum number ($n$).

sections.title: Explanation of Line Spectrum of Hydrogen

subsections.title: Energy Change during Transitions
subsections.content_text: Bohr’s model explains the line spectrum of hydrogen quantitatively. Energy is absorbed when an electron moves from a lower orbit ($n_i$) to a higher orbit ($n_f$), and emitted when it moves from a higher orbit to a lower orbit. The energy gap is: $\Delta E = E_f - E_i$ (Eq. 2.16). Substituting the energy expression: $\Delta E = R_H (\frac{1}{n_i^2} - \frac{1}{n_f^2}) = 2.18 \times 10^{-18} J (\frac{1}{n_i^2} - \frac{1}{n_f^2})$ (Eq. 2.17).

subsections.title: Frequency and Wavenumber Calculations

worked_examples.label: Example 2.10
worked_examples.problem: What are the frequency and wavelength of a photon emitted during a transition from $n = 5$ state to the $n = 2$ state in the hydrogen atom?
worked_examples.solution: Since $n_i = 5$ and $n_f = 2$, this transition gives rise to a spectral line in the visible region of the Balmer series. From equation (2.17): $\Delta E = 2.18 \times 10^{-18} J [\frac{1}{5^2} - \frac{1}{2^2}] = -4.58 \times 10^{-19}$ J. It is an emission energy. The frequency of the photon is: $\nu = \frac{\Delta E}{h} = \frac{4.58 \times 10^{-19} J}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} Js} = 6.91 \times 10^{14}$ Hz. The wavelength is: $\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8 m s^{-1}}{6.91 \times 10^{14} Hz} = 434$ nm.

worked_examples.label: Example 2.11
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the energy associated with the first orbit of $He^+$. What is the radius of this orbit?

subsections.title: Absorption vs Emission Spectra
subsections.content_text: In absorption spectra ($n_f > n_i$), $\Delta E$ is positive and energy is absorbed. In emission spectra ($n_i > n_f$), $\Delta E$ is negative and energy is released. The brightness or intensity of spectral lines depends on the number of photons of the same wavelength or frequency absorbed or emitted.

subsections.title: Shortcomings of the Bohr Model
subsections.content_text: Bohr’s model fails in several areas: i) It cannot account for the finer details (doublets) of the hydrogen spectrum or explain the spectra of multi-electron atoms like helium. ii) It cannot explain the splitting of spectral lines in magnetic fields (Zeeman effect) or electric fields (Stark effect). iii) It fails to explain how atoms form molecules through chemical bonds.

sections.title: TOWARDS QUANTUM MECHANICAL MODEL OF THE ATOM

subsections.title: Introduction to Quantum Mechanical Model
subsections.content_text: Due to the limitations of Bohr's model, a more general model was needed. Two key developments led to the formulation of the quantum mechanical model: 1. Dual behaviour of matter, and 2. Heisenberg uncertainty principle.

subsections.title: de Broglie's Hypothesis
subsections.content_text: In 1924, Louis de Broglie proposed that matter exhibits dual behaviour, possessing both particle and wavelike properties. Just as photons have momentum and wavelength, electrons should also have these properties. He proposed a relation between wavelength ($\lambda$) and momentum ($p$) of a material particle. $$\lambda = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{h}{p} \quad (2.22)$$ where $m$ is the mass of the particle, $v$ its velocity and $p$ its momentum. de Broglie’s prediction was confirmed experimentally when it was found that an electron beam undergoes diffraction, a phenomenon characteristic of waves.

subsections.title: Experimental Confirmation and Electron Microscope
subsections.content_text: The wavelike behaviour of electrons has been put to use in making an electron microscope, which is based on the wave nature of electrons just as an ordinary microscope utilises the wave nature of light. An electron microscope is a powerful tool in modern scientific research because it achieves a magnification of about 15 million times.

subsections.title: Wave Nature of Macroscopic vs Microscopic Objects
subsections.content_text: According to de Broglie, every object in motion has a wave character. The wavelengths associated with ordinary objects are so short (because of their large masses) that their wave properties cannot be detected. The wavelengths associated with electrons and other subatomic particles (with very small mass) can however be detected experimentally. Results obtained from the following problems prove these points qualitatively.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Wavelength of a Macroscopic Object
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the de Broglie wavelength for a macroscopic object (a ball) to demonstrate why its wave properties are undetectable.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.12
worked_examples.problem: What will be the wavelength of a ball of mass 0.1 kg moving with a velocity of 10 m s$^{-1}$?
worked_examples.solution: According to de Broglie equation (2.22): $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}}{(0.1 \text{ kg})(10 \text{ ms}^{-1})} = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ m}$ (since $\text{J} = \text{kg m}^2 \text{s}^{-2}$).

subsections.title: Worked Example: Wavelength of an Electron
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the de Broglie wavelength for a subatomic particle (electron) given its kinetic energy.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.13
worked_examples.problem: The mass of an electron is $9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg. If its K.E. is $3.0 \times 10^{-25}$ J, calculate its wavelength.
worked_examples.solution: Since $\text{K.E.} = \frac{1}{2} mv^2$, $v = \left(\frac{2\text{K.E.}}{m}\right)^{1/2} = \left(\frac{2 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg m}^2 \text{s}^{-2}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}}\right)^{1/2} = 812 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. Using de Broglie equation: $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}}{(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg})(812 \text{ ms}^{-1})} = 8967 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} = 896.7 \text{ nm}$.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Mass of a Photon
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates how to calculate the mass of a photon based on its wavelength using the de Broglie relation.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.14
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the mass of a photon with wavelength 3.6 Å.
worked_examples.solution: $\lambda = 3.6 \text{ Å} = 3.6 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$. Velocity of photon = velocity of light ($c$). $m = \frac{h}{\lambda v} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}}{(3.6 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m})(3 \times 10^8 \text{ ms}^{-1})} = 6.135 \times 10^{-29} \text{ kg}$.

subsections.title: Biographical Note: Louis de Broglie
subsections.content_text: Louis de Broglie (1892–1987) was a French physicist who proposed the wave-particle duality of matter in 1924. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1929.

diagrams.label: Louis de Broglie
diagrams.description: Portrait of Louis de Broglie, the physicist who proposed the wavelike properties of matter.

subsections.title: Definition and Mathematical Statement
subsections.content_text: Werner Heisenberg stated that it is impossible to determine simultaneously, the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron. Mathematically, it is given as: $$\Delta x \times \Delta p_x \ge \frac{h}{4\pi} \quad (2.23)$$ or $$\Delta x \times \Delta(mv_x) \ge \frac{h}{4\pi}$$ or $$\Delta x \times \Delta v_x \ge \frac{h}{4\pi m}$$ where $\Delta x$ is the uncertainty in position and $\Delta p_x$ (or $\Delta v_x$) is the uncertainty in momentum (or velocity) of the particle.

subsections.title: Physical Interpretation and Measurement Constraints
subsections.content_text: If the position of the electron is known with high degree of accuracy ($\Delta x$ is small), then the velocity will be uncertain ($\Delta v_x$ is large), and vice versa. To observe an electron, we must illuminate it with light having a wavelength smaller than the dimensions of an electron. However, high momentum photons of such light ($p = h/\lambda$) change the energy and velocity of the electron upon collision, making the velocity uncertain.

subsections.title: Significance and Trajectories
subsections.content_text: The principle rules out the existence of definite paths or trajectories for electrons. A trajectory is defined by knowing both position and velocity at every instant. Since these cannot be determined simultaneously for sub-atomic objects, it is not possible to talk of the trajectory of an electron.

subsections.title: Microscopic vs Macroscopic Scale
subsections.content_text: The effect of the Uncertainty Principle is significant only for microscopic objects and negligible for macroscopic ones. For a milligram ($10^{-6}$ kg) object, $\Delta v \cdot \Delta x \approx 10^{-28} \text{ m}^2\text{s}^{-1}$, which is insignificant. For an electron ($9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg), $\Delta v \cdot \Delta x \approx 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{s}^{-1}$. If $\Delta x = 10^{-8}$ m, then $\Delta v \approx 10^4 \text{ ms}^{-1}$, which is large enough to invalidate the classical picture of fixed orbits.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Uncertainty in Electron Velocity
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the uncertainty in velocity when the position of an electron is constrained by a microscope measurement.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.15
worked_examples.problem: A microscope using suitable photons is employed to locate an electron in an atom within a distance of 0.1 Å. What is the uncertainty involved in the measurement of its velocity?
worked_examples.solution: $\Delta x = 0.1 \text{ Å} = 10^{-11} \text{ m}$. $\Delta v = \frac{h}{4\pi m \Delta x} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg} \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}} = 5.79 \times 10^6 \text{ ms}^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Uncertainty in Macroscopic Position
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the uncertainty in position for a macroscopic object (golf ball) to show its insignificance.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.16
worked_examples.problem: A golf ball has a mass of 40g, and a speed of 45 m/s. If the speed can be measured within accuracy of 2%, calculate the uncertainty in the position.
worked_examples.solution: $m = 40 \text{ g} = 0.04 \text{ kg}$. $\Delta v = 2\% \text{ of } 45 = 0.9 \text{ ms}^{-1}$. $\Delta x = \frac{h}{4\pi m \Delta v} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 0.04 \text{ kg} \times 0.9 \text{ ms}^{-1}} = 1.46 \times 10^{-33} \text{ m}$. This is nearly $10^{18}$ times smaller than the diameter of a typical atomic nucleus.

subsections.title: Biographical Note: Werner Heisenberg
subsections.content_text: Werner Heisenberg (1901–1976) was a German physicist who received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1932 for the creation of quantum mechanics and the formulation of the uncertainty principle.

diagrams.label: Werner Heisenberg
diagrams.description: Portrait of Werner Heisenberg, the physicist who formulated the Uncertainty Principle.

sections.title: Reasons for the Failure of the Bohr Model

subsections.title: Limitations regarding Wave Nature and Uncertainty
subsections.content_text: The Bohr model fails because it treats the electron as a particle moving in well-defined circular orbits, ignoring its wave character. Furthermore, an orbit implies a clearly defined path where both position and velocity are known exactly, which contradicts the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle. Consequently, the Bohr model ignores the dual behaviour of matter and the fundamental limits of measurement. In the quantum mechanical model, precise statements of position and momentum are replaced by statements of probability.

subsections.title: Transition to Quantum Mechanics
subsections.content_text: In view of the inherent weaknesses in the Bohr model, such as its inability to account for wave-particle duality and the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, there was no point in extending it to other atoms. A new insight into atomic structure was required that could reconcile these concepts and account for the behavior of sub-atomic particles. This transition was marked by the advent of quantum mechanics, which provides a more comprehensive framework for understanding the atom.

subsections.title: Introduction to Quantum Mechanics
subsections.content_text: Classical mechanics, based on Newton’s laws of motion, successfully describes the motion of macroscopic objects (like falling stones or orbiting planets) which exhibit particle-like behavior. However, it fails for microscopic objects like electrons and atoms because it ignores the dual behavior of matter and the uncertainty principle. Quantum mechanics is a theoretical science that deals with the study of microscopic objects that have both observable wave-like and particle-like properties. It specifies the laws of motion that these objects obey. When applied to macroscopic objects where wave properties are insignificant, the results of quantum mechanics align with classical mechanics. It was developed independently in 1926 by Werner Heisenberg and Erwin Schrödinger.

diagrams.label: Erwin Schrödinger (1887-1961)
diagrams.description: Portrait of Erwin Schrödinger, an Austrian physicist who received his Ph.D. in theoretical physics in 1910 and shared the Nobel Prize for Physics with P.A.M. Dirac in 1933 for the development of quantum mechanics.

subsections.title: The Schrödinger Equation
subsections.content_text:
The fundamental equation of quantum mechanics was developed by Schrödinger. For a system (such as an atom or a molecule) whose energy does not change with time, the Schrödinger equation is written as: $$\hat{H}\psi = E\psi$$ In this equation, $\hat{H}$ is a mathematical operator called the Hamiltonian. Schrödinger gave a recipe for constructing this operator from the expression for the total energy of the system. The total energy takes into account the kinetic energies of all sub-atomic particles (electrons, nuclei), the attractive potential between electrons and nuclei, and the repulsive potential among electrons and nuclei individually. Solving this equation yields the total energy $E$ and the wave function $\psi$.
When the Schrödinger equation is solved for the hydrogen atom, the solution gives the possible energy levels the electron can occupy and the corresponding wave functions ($\psi$) associated with each level. These quantized energy states and wave functions are characterized by a set of three quantum numbers: the principal quantum number $n$, the azimuthal quantum number $l$, and the magnetic quantum number $m_l$. These arise as a natural consequence of the solution. The wave function $\psi$ is a mathematical function whose value depends on the coordinates of the electron and contains all information about it, though $\psi$ itself has no physical meaning. Such wave functions for one-electron systems are called atomic orbitals.

subsections.title: Multi-electron Atoms and Approximate Methods
subsections.content_text: Applying the Schrödinger equation to multi-electron atoms presents a difficulty because it cannot be solved exactly. This is overcome by using approximate methods with the aid of modern computers. These calculations show that orbitals in multi-electron atoms do not differ radically from hydrogen orbitals. The principal difference lies in the increased nuclear charge, which causes orbitals to contract. Furthermore, while the energy of hydrogen orbitals depends only on the principal quantum number $n$, the energies of orbitals in multi-electron atoms depend on both $n$ and the azimuthal quantum number $l$.

subsections.title: Important Features of the Quantum Mechanical Model
subsections.content_text: The quantum mechanical model of the atom, emerging from the Schrödinger equation, has several key features: 1. The energy of electrons in atoms is quantized (can only have specific values). 2. Quantized electronic energy levels result from the wave-like properties of electrons. 3. Due to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, the exact position and velocity of an electron cannot be determined simultaneously; we only talk about the probability of finding an electron. 4. An atomic orbital is the wave function $\psi$ for an electron. An orbital can contain at most two electrons. In multi-electron atoms, electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy. 5. The probability of finding an electron at a point is proportional to the square of the wave function, $|\psi|^2$, known as probability density. This allows us to predict regions around the nucleus where the electron will most probably be found.

subsections.title: Qualitative Description of Orbitals
subsections.content_text: A large number of orbitals are possible in an atom, distinguished by their size, shape, and orientation. A smaller orbital size means a higher probability of finding the electron near the nucleus. Shape and orientation indicate the probability of finding the electron along specific directions. Atomic orbitals are precisely labeled by three quantum numbers: $n, l,$ and $m_l$.

subsections.title: The Principal Quantum Number ($n$)
subsections.content_text: The principal quantum number '$n$' is a positive integer with values $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$. It determines the size and, to a large extent, the energy of the orbital. For hydrogen and hydrogen-like species ($He^+, Li^{2+}$, etc.), energy and size depend only on $n$. It also identifies the shell. As $n$ increases, the number of allowed orbitals increases and is given by $n^2$. All orbitals of a given value of $n$ constitute a single shell, represented by letters: $n=1 (K), n=2 (L), n=3 (M), n=4 (N)$. Size and distance from the nucleus increase with $n$, as does the energy required to shift the electron away from the nucleus.

subsections.title: The Azimuthal Quantum Number ($l$)
subsections.content_text: The azimuthal quantum number '$l$', also known as orbital angular momentum or subsidiary quantum number, defines the three-dimensional shape of the orbital. For a given $n$, $l$ can have $n$ values ranging from $0$ to $n-1$. Each shell consists of one or more sub-shells or sub-levels, where the number of sub-shells equals $n$. Sub-shells are represented by symbols: $l=0 (s), l=1 (p), l=2 (d), l=3 (f), l=4 (g), l=5 (h)$. For example, in the first shell ($n=1$), there is only one sub-shell ($l=0$). In the second shell ($n=2$), there are two sub-shells ($l=0, 1$).

subsections.title: The Magnetic Orbital Quantum Number ($m_l$)
subsections.content_text: The magnetic orbital quantum number '$m_l$' gives information about the spatial orientation of the orbital with respect to a standard set of coordinate axes. For any sub-shell (defined by $l$), there are $2l+1$ possible values of $m_l$, ranging from $-l$ to $+l$ including zero: $m_l = -l, -(l-1), -(l-2) \dots 0 \dots (l-2), (l-1), l$. For example, for $l=0$ (s orbital), $m_l=0$ (one orbital). For $l=1$ (p sub-shell), $m_l = -1, 0, +1$ (three p orbitals). For $l=2$ (d sub-shell), $m_l = -2, -1, 0, +1, +2$ (five d orbitals). The values of $m_l$ are derived from $l$, which in turn are derived from $n$.

subsections.title: Relation between Subshell and Number of Orbitals
subsections.content_text: Each orbital in an atom is defined by a set of values for $n, l$ and $m_l$. For instance, an orbital described by the quantum numbers $n = 2, l = 1, m_l = 0$ is an orbital in the $p$ subshell of the second shell. The relationship between the subshell and the number of orbitals associated with it is determined by the value of $l$, where each subshell contains $2l + 1$ orbitals.

tables.label: Table 2.x
tables.caption: Relation between the subshell and the number of orbitals

subsections.title: Electron Spin Quantum Number ($m_s$)
subsections.content_text: The three quantum numbers ($n, l, m_l$) define an orbital's energy, shape, and orientation but are insufficient to explain the line spectra of multi-electron atoms, which often show doublets or triplets. In 1925, George Uhlenbeck and Samuel Goudsmit proposed the electron spin quantum number ($m_s$). An electron spins around its own axis, possessing an intrinsic spin angular momentum. This vector quantity has two orientations relative to a chosen axis, distinguished by $m_s$ values of $+1/2$ or $-1/2$. These are called the two spin states of the electron, represented by arrows $\uparrow$ (spin up) and $\downarrow$ (spin down). Electrons with different $m_s$ values have opposite spins. An orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons, which must have opposite spins.

subsections.title: Summary of Quantum Numbers
subsections.content_text: The four quantum numbers provide essential information about electrons: i) $n$ defines the shell, size, and largely the energy of the orbital. ii) $l$ identifies the subshell and determines the shape of the orbital; there are $n$ subshells in the $n^{th}$ shell, and each subshell has $(2l+1)$ orbitals. iii) $m_l$ designates the orientation of the orbital, with $(2l+1)$ values for a given $l$. iv) $m_s$ refers to the orientation of the electron's spin.

subsections.title: Comparison of Orbit and Orbital
subsections.content_text: Orbit and orbital are not synonymous. An orbit is a circular path proposed by Bohr, but Heisenberg's uncertainty principle makes such a precise path impossible to describe, rendering Bohr orbits physically meaningless. An atomic orbital is a quantum mechanical concept referring to the wave function $\psi$. While $\psi$ has no physical meaning, $|\psi|^2$ represents the probability density. The product of $|\psi|^2$ and a volume element gives the probability of finding an electron in that volume. The total probability is the sum of these products over a given volume, allowing for the determination of the electron's probable distribution.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Total Number of Orbitals
subsections.content_text: This example illustrates how to determine the total number of orbitals for a specific principal quantum number $n$ by summing the orbitals in each possible subshell or by using the $n^2$ relationship.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.17
worked_examples.problem: What is the total number of orbitals associated with the principal quantum number $n = 3$?
worked_examples.solution: For $n = 3$, the possible values of $l$ are 0, 1 and 2. Thus there is one $3s$ orbital ($n = 3, l = 0, m_l = 0$); three $3p$ orbitals ($n = 3, l = 1, m_l = -1, 0, +1$); and five $3d$ orbitals ($n = 3, l = 2, m_l = -2, -1, 0, +1, +2$). Total orbitals = $1 + 3 + 5 = 9$. Alternatively, number of orbitals $= n^2 = 3^2 = 9$.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Orbital Notation
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates the use of $s, p, d, f$ notations to describe orbitals based on their principal ($n$) and azimuthal ($l$) quantum numbers.

worked_examples.label: Problem 2.18
worked_examples.problem: Using $s, p, d, f$ notations, describe the orbital with the following quantum numbers: (a) $n = 2, l = 1$, (b) $n = 4, l = 0$, (c) $n = 5, l = 3$, (d) $n = 3, l = 2$
worked_examples.solution: The orbital notations are: (a) $n=2, l=1$ is $2p$; (b) $n=4, l=0$ is $4s$; (c) $n=5, l=3$ is $5f$; (d) $n=3, l=2$ is $3d$.

subsections.title: Wave Function and Probability Density Plots
subsections.content_text: The orbital wave function $\psi$ is a mathematical function of electron coordinates with no direct physical meaning. However, plots of $\psi$ and its square $|\psi|^2$ (probability density) against distance $r$ from the nucleus provide insights into electron distribution. For a $1s$ orbital, probability density is highest at the nucleus and decays with distance. For a $2s$ orbital, it decreases to zero (a node) and then increases to a smaller maximum before decaying.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.12
diagrams.description: Plots of (a) orbital wave function $\psi(r)$ and (b) probability density $\psi^2(r)$ for $1s$ and $2s$ orbitals. The $1s$ plots show a monotonic decrease, while the $2s$ plots show a zero-crossing in $\psi$ and a corresponding zero-point in $\psi^2$ before a second peak.

subsections.title: Nodal Surfaces (Nodes)
subsections.content_text: Nodal surfaces, or nodes, are regions where the probability density function $|\psi|^2$ is zero. For any $ns$-orbital, the number of nodes is given by $(n - 1)$. Thus, a $1s$ orbital has zero nodes, a $2s$ orbital has one node, and a $3s$ orbital has two nodes.

subsections.title: Charge Cloud and Boundary Surface Diagrams for s-orbitals
subsections.content_text: Electron distribution can be visualized using charge cloud diagrams (dot density) or boundary surface diagrams (contour surfaces). Boundary surface diagrams typically enclose a 90% probability region, as a 100% region is impossible to define. $s$-orbitals are spherically symmetric, meaning probability is identical in all directions at a fixed distance. The size of $s$-orbitals increases with $n$ ($4s > 3s > 2s > 1s$), placing the electron further from the nucleus in higher shells.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.13
diagrams.description: (a) Charge cloud diagrams for $1s$ and $2s$ orbitals showing dot density. (b) Spherical boundary surface diagrams for $1s$ and $2s$ orbitals, illustrating the size difference.

subsections.title: Boundary Surface Diagrams for p-orbitals
subsections.content_text: Boundary surface diagrams for $p$ orbitals ($l = 1$) are not spherical but consist of two lobes on opposite sides of a plane passing through the nucleus. There are three $p$ orbitals ($p_x, p_y, p_z$) oriented along the Cartesian axes. The probability density The probability density function is zero on the plane where the two lobes touch each other. The size, shape and energy of the three $p$ orbitals are identical; they differ only in their orientation. Since the lobes are oriented along the $x, y,$ or $z$ axis, they are designated as $2p_x, 2p_y,$ and $2p_z$. There is no simple relation between the values of $m_l$ ($-1, 0,$ and $+1$) and the specific Cartesian directions. Because there are three possible values of $m_l$, there are three $p$ orbitals whose axes are mutually perpendicular. Like $s$ orbitals, $p$ orbitals increase in size and energy with an increase in the principal quantum number ($n$); thus, the order of energy and size is $4p > 3p > 2p$. The probability density functions for $p$ orbitals also pass through zero at specific distances from the nucleus, creating radial nodes.
