The number of radial nodes for a $p$ orbital is given by the formula $n - 2$. For example, a $3p$ orbital has 1 radial node, while a $4p$ orbital has 2.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.14
diagrams.description: Boundary surface diagrams of the three $2p$ orbitals ($2p_x, 2p_y, 2p_z$), each showing two lobes oriented along the respective axis with the nucleus at the origin.

subsections.title: Boundary Surface Diagrams for d-orbitals
subsections.content_text: For the azimuthal quantum number $l = 2$, the orbitals are known as $d$-orbitals. The minimum value of the principal quantum number ($n$) for these orbitals is 3, as $l$ cannot exceed $n-1$. There are five possible $m_l$ values ($-2, -1, 0, +1, +2$), resulting in five $d$ orbitals designated as $d_{xy}, d_{yz}, d_{xz}, d_{x^2-y^2},$ and $d_{z^2}$. The shapes of the first four $d$-orbitals are similar (cloverleaf-like), while the $d_{z^2}$ orbital has a unique shape consisting of two lobes along the $z$-axis and a central ring in the $xy$-plane. All five $3d$ orbitals are equivalent in energy (degenerate). Orbitals with $n > 3$ (like $4d, 5d$) have similar shapes but differ in size and energy.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.15
diagrams.description: Boundary surface diagrams of the five 3d orbitals: (a) dxy, (b) dxz, (c) dyz, (d) dx2-y2, and (e) dz2. The diagrams show the orientation of lobes relative to the Cartesian axes, with dz2 showing a distinct doughnut-shaped ring.

subsections.title: Nodes in Orbitals
subsections.content_text: In addition to radial nodes, $np$ and $nd$ orbitals possess angular nodes where the probability density is zero on planes passing through the nucleus. For a $p_z$ orbital, the $xy$-plane is a nodal plane. For a $d_{xy}$ orbital, there are two nodal planes passing through the origin and bisecting the $xy$-plane. The number of angular nodes is equal to the azimuthal quantum number $l$. The number of radial nodes is given by $(n - l - 1)$. The total number of nodes in an orbital is the sum of angular and radial nodes, which simplifies to $(n - 1)$.

subsections.title: Orbital Energies in Hydrogen Atom
subsections.content_text: In a hydrogen atom, the energy of an electron is determined solely by the principal quantum number ($n$). Consequently, all orbitals with the same value of $n$ have the same energy, regardless of their shape ($l$). Such orbitals are called degenerate. The energy sequence for hydrogen is: $1s < 2s = 2p < 3s = 3p = 3d < 4s = 4p = 4d = 4f < \dots$. The $1s$ orbital represents the most stable condition, known as the ground state. Electrons in higher energy orbitals are said to be in an excited state.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.16
diagrams.description: Energy level diagrams comparing (a) the hydrogen atom, where all subshells of the same principal quantum number are at the same energy level, and (b) multi-electron atoms, where subshells (s, p, d) within the same shell have different energy levels.

subsections.title: Orbital Energies in Multi-electron Atoms
subsections.content_text: In multi-electron atoms, orbital energy depends on both $n$ and $l$. For a given $n$, the energy increases in the order $s < p < d < f$. This splitting occurs because of mutual repulsion among electrons. While the primary interaction in hydrogen is the attraction between the nucleus and the single electron, multi-electron atoms involve both nuclear attraction and electron-electron repulsion. Inner-shell electrons partially screen or shield outer-shell electrons from the full positive charge of the nucleus ($Ze$). This reduced charge experienced by outer electrons is called the effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff} e$).

subsections.title: The (n+l) Rule and Shielding Effects
subsections.content_text: The extent of shielding depends on the shape of the orbital. Spherical $s$ orbitals shield more effectively than $p$ orbitals, which in turn shield more than $d$ orbitals. Because $s$ electrons spend more time near the nucleus, they are more tightly bound and have lower energy. The relative energies of orbitals are governed by the $(n + l)$ rule: 1. The lower the value of $(n + l)$, the lower the energy of the orbital. 2. If two orbitals have the same $(n + l)$ value, the one with the lower $n$ value has the lower energy. Additionally, the energy of orbitals in the same subshell decreases as the atomic number ($Z$) increases due to increased $Z_{eff}$; for example, $E_{2s}(H) > E_{2s}(Li) > E_{2s}(Na) > E_{2s}(K)$.

tables.label: Table 2.5
tables.caption: Arrangement of Orbitals with Increasing Energy on the Basis of (n+l) Rule

subsections.title: Principles of Electron Filling
subsections.content_text: The distribution of electrons into the orbitals of an atom is governed by three main rules: the Aufbau principle, the Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund’s rule of maximum multiplicity. These rules ensure electrons are filled in a way that minimizes the total energy of the atom.

subsections.title: Aufbau Principle
subsections.content_text: The word 'aufbau' is German for 'building up'. The Aufbau principle states that in the ground state of an atom, orbitals are filled in order of their increasing energies. Electrons first occupy the lowest energy orbital available and move to higher energy orbitals only after the lower ones are filled. The building up of orbitals means the filling up of orbitals with electrons. The principle states : In the ground state of the atoms, the orbitals are filled in order of their increasing energies. In other words, electrons first occupy the lowest energy orbital available and move to higher energy orbitals only after the lower energy orbitals are filled. As you have learnt above, energy of a given orbital depends upon effective nuclear charge and different type of orbitals are affected to different extent. Thus, there is no single ordering of energies of orbitals which will be universally correct for all atoms. However, following order of energies of the orbitals is extremely useful: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p, 5s, 4d, 5p, 6s, 4f, 5d, 6p, 7s... The order may be remembered by using the method given in Fig. 2.17. Starting from the top, the direction of the arrows gives the order of filling of orbitals, that is starting from right top to bottom left. With respect to placement of outermost valence electrons, it is remarkably accurate for all atoms. For example, valence electron in potassium must choose between 3d and 4s orbitals and as predicted by this sequence, it is found in 4s orbital. The above order should be assumed to be a rough guide to the filling of energy levels. In many cases, the orbitals are similar in energy and small changes in atomic structure may bring about a change in the order of filling. Even then, the above series is a useful guide to the building of the electronic structure of an atom provided that it is remembered that exceptions may occur.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.17
diagrams.description: Order of filling of orbitals. The diagram shows the sequence of orbital filling (1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p, 5s, 4d, 5p, 6s, 4f, 5d, 6p, 7s) using diagonal arrows through a grid of orbitals.

subsections.title: Pauli Exclusion Principle
subsections.content_text: The number of electrons to be filled in various orbitals is restricted by the exclusion principle, given by the Austrian scientist Wolfgang Pauli (1926). According to this principle : No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers. Pauli exclusion principle can also be stated as : “Only two electrons may exist in the same orbital and these electrons must have opposite spin.” This means that the two electrons can have the same value of three quantum numbers $n$, $l$ and $m_l$, but must have the opposite spin quantum number. The restriction imposed by Pauli’s exclusion principle on the number of electrons in an orbital helps in calculating the capacity of electrons to be present in any subshell. For example, subshell 1s comprises one orbital and thus the maximum number of electrons present in 1s subshell can be two, in p and d subshells, the maximum number of electrons can be 6 and 10 and so on. This can be summed up as : the maximum number of electrons in the shell with principal quantum number $n$ is equal to $2n^2$.

subsections.title: Hund’s Rule of Maximum Multiplicity
subsections.content_text: This rule deals with the filling of electrons into the orbitals belonging to the same subshell (that is, orbitals of equal energy, called degenerate orbitals). It states : pairing of electrons in the orbitals belonging to the same subshell (p, d or f) does not take place until each orbital belonging to that subshell has got one electron each i.e., it is singly occupied. Since there are three p, five d and seven f orbitals, therefore, the pairing of electrons will start in the p, d and f orbitals with the entry of 4th, 6th and 8th electron, respectively. It has been observed that half filled and fully filled degenerate set of orbitals acquire extra stability due to their symmetry (see Section, 2.6.7).

subsections.title: Definition and Notation
subsections.content_text: The distribution of electrons into orbitals of an atom is called its electronic configuration. If one keeps in mind the basic rules which govern the filling of different atomic orbitals, the electronic configurations of different atoms can be written very easily. The electronic configuration of different atoms can be represented in two ways. For example : (i) $s^a p^b d^c$ ...... notation (ii) Orbital diagram. In the first notation, the subshell is represented by the respective letter symbol and the number of electrons present in the subshell is depicted, as the super script, like a, b, c, ... etc. The similar subshell represented for different shells is differentiated by writing the principal quantum number before the respective subshell. In the second notation each orbital of the subshell is represented by a box and the electron is represented by an arrow ($\uparrow$) a positive spin or an arrow ($\downarrow$) a negative spin. The advantage of second notation over the first is that it represents all the four quantum numbers.

subsections.title: Configurations of First Ten Elements
subsections.content_text: The hydrogen atom has only one electron which goes in the orbital with the lowest energy, namely 1s. The electronic configuration of the hydrogen atom is $1s^1$. The second electron in helium (He) can also occupy the 1s orbital, giving the configuration $1s^2$. The two electrons differ from each other with opposite spin. The third electron of lithium (Li) is not allowed in the 1s orbital because of Pauli exclusion principle; it takes the next available choice, the 2s orbital, resulting in $1s^2 2s^1$. Beryllium (Be) is $1s^2 2s^2$. In the next six elements—boron (B), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), fluorine (F) and neon (Ne)—the 2p orbitals get progressively filled, ending with neon at $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$.

tables.caption: Orbital diagrams for H and He

tables.label: Table 2.2
tables.caption: Orbital picture of elements Li to Ne

subsections.title: Core Electrons, Valence Electrons, and Period 3
subsections.content_text: The electronic configuration of elements sodium (Na, $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$) to argon (Ar, $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6$) follow the same pattern as lithium to neon, filling the 3s and 3p orbitals. This can be simplified using noble gas notation: (Na, $[Ne] 3s^1$) to (Ar, $[Ne] 3s^2 3p^6$). Electrons in completely filled shells are core electrons, while those in the shell with the highest principal quantum number are valence electrons. In potassium (K) and calcium (Ca), the 4s orbital is filled before the 3d because it is lower in energy.

subsections.title: Transition Elements and Anomalous Configurations
subsections.content_text: Beginning with scandium (Sc), the five 3d orbitals are progressively filled. In the next ten elements (Sc to Zn), the 3d orbitals are occupied. Chromium (Cr) and copper (Cu) have anomalous configurations: $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$ and $[Ar] 3d^{10} 4s^1$ respectively, instead of the expected $3d^4 4s^2$ and $3d^9 4s^2$. This is because fully filled and half-filled orbitals ($p^3, p^6, d^5, d^{10}, f^7, f^{14}$) have extra stability due to their symmetry.

subsections.title: Filling of Higher Orbitals and General Trends
subsections.content_text: After 3d saturation, the 4p orbital fills from gallium (Ga) to krypton (Kr). The pattern continues with 5s, 4d, and 5p (Rb to Xe), then 6s (Cs, Ba), followed by 4f and 5d (La to Hg), and finally 6p, 7s, 5f, and 6d. Elements after uranium (U) are short-lived and synthetic. Electronic configurations of known elements are determined by spectroscopic methods and tabulated in Table 2.6.

sections.title: Stability of Completely Filled and Half Filled Subshells

subsections.title: Energy Considerations and Stability
subsections.content_text: The ground state electronic configuration of an atom always corresponds to the state of the lowest total electronic energy. In elements like Cu or Cr, where the 4s and 3d subshells differ slightly in energy, an electron shifts from the lower energy 4s to the higher energy 3d subshell if it results in a half-filled or completely filled state. The valence configurations of Cr and Cu are $3d^5 4s^1$ and $3d^{10} 4s^1$ respectively, providing extra stability associated with these symmetric configurations. The completely filled and half-filled subshells are stable due to the following reasons: 1. Symmetrical distribution of electrons: It is well known that symmetry leads to stability. The completely filled or half filled subshells have symmetrical distribution of electrons in them and are therefore more stable. Electrons in the same subshell (here $3d$) have equal energy but different spatial distribution. Consequently, their shielding of one-another is relatively small and the electrons are more strongly attracted by the nucleus.

subsections.title: Exchange Energy
subsections.content_text: 2. Exchange Energy: The stabilizing effect arises whenever two or more electrons with the same spin are present in the degenerate orbitals of a subshell. These electrons tend to exchange their positions and the energy released due to this exchange is called exchange energy. The number of exchanges that can take place is maximum when the subshell is either half filled or completely filled (Fig. 2.18). As a result the exchange energy is maximum and so is the stability.

diagrams.label: Fig. 2.18
diagrams.description: Possible exchange for a $d^5$ configuration. The diagram illustrates the concept of exchange energy by showing the possible exchanges of electrons with parallel spins in five degenerate orbitals. For the first electron, there are 4 possible exchanges. For the second electron, there are 3 possible exchanges. For the third electron, there are 2 possible exchanges. For the fourth electron, there is 1 possible exchange. The total number of exchanges for a $d^5$ configuration is 10, which contributes to its extra stability.

subsections.title: Basis of Hund's Rule and Stability Summary
subsections.content_text: The exchange energy is at the basis of Hund’s rule that electrons which enter orbitals of equal energy have parallel spins as far as possible. In other words, the extra stability of half-filled and completely filled subshell is due to: (i) relatively small shielding, (ii) smaller coulombic repulsion energy, and (iii) larger exchange energy. Details about the exchange energy will be dealt with in higher classes.

subsections.title: Electronic Configurations of the Elements
subsections.content_text: Table 2.6 provides a comprehensive list of the electronic configurations of elements from Hydrogen ($Z=1$) to Roentgenium ($Z=111$). The table details the distribution of electrons across subshells from $1s$ to $7s$. Elements marked with an asterisk (*) exhibit exceptional electronic configurations, often deviating from the Aufbau principle to achieve the stability of half-filled or fully-filled subshells (e.g., Cr, Cu, Nb, Mo, Pd, Ag, La, Ce, Gd, Pt, Au, Ac, Th, Pa, U, Np, Cm, Bk, Lr). Double asterisks (**) indicate elements that have been reported but not yet fully authenticated and named.

items.problem: How many electrons in an atom may have the following quantum numbers? (a) $n = 4, m_s = -1/2$ (b) $n = 3, l = 0$
Show that the circumference of the Bohr orbit for the hydrogen atom is an integral multiple of the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving around the orbit.

If the diameter of a carbon atom is 0.15 nm, calculate the number of carbon atoms which can be placed side by side in a straight line across length of scale of length 20 cm long.
$2 \times 10^8$ atoms of carbon are arranged side by side. Calculate the radius of carbon atom if the length of this arrangement is 2.4 cm.
The diameter of zinc atom is 2.6 \AA. Calculate (a) radius of zinc atom in pm and (b) number of atoms present in a length of 1.6 cm if the zinc atoms are arranged side by side lengthwise.
A certain particle carries $2.5 \times 10^{-16}$ C of static electric charge. Calculate the number of electrons present in it.

In Milikan’s experiment, static electric charge on the oil drops has been obtained by shining X-rays. If the static electric charge on the oil drop is $-1.282 \times 10^{-18}$ C, calculate the number of electrons present on it.

In Rutherford’s experiment, generally the thin foil of heavy atoms, like gold, platinum etc. have been used to be bombarded by the $\alpha$-particles. If the thin foil of light atoms like aluminium etc. is used, what difference would be observed from the above results?
Symbols $^{79}_{35}Br$ and $^{79}Br$ can be written, whereas symbols $^{35}_{79}Br$ and $^{35}Br$ are not acceptable. Answer briefly.
An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7% more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
An ion with mass number 37 possesses one unit of negative charge. If the ion contains 11.1% more neutrons than the electrons, find the symbol of the ion.
An ion with mass number 56 contains 3 units of positive charge and 30.4% more neutrons than electrons. Assign the symbol to this ion.

Arrange the following type of radiations in increasing order of frequency: (a) radiation from microwave oven (b) amber light from traffic signal (c) radiation from FM radio (d) cosmic rays from outer space and (e) X-rays.
Nitrogen laser produces a radiation at a wavelength of 337.1 nm. If the number of photons emitted is $5.6 \times 10^{24}$, calculate the power of this laser.

Neon gas is generally used in the sign boards. If it emits strongly at 616 nm, calculate (a) the frequency of emission, (b) distance traveled by this radiation in 30 s (c) energy of quantum and (d) number of quanta present if it produces 2 J of energy.

In astronomical observations, signals observed from the distant stars are generally weak. If the photon detector receives a total of $3.15 \times 10^{-18}$ J from the radiations of 600 nm, calculate the number of photons received by the detector.

Lifetimes of the molecules in the excited states are often measured by using pulsed radiation source of duration nearly in the nano second range. If the radiation source has the duration of 2 ns and the number of photons emitted during the pulse source is $2.5 \times 10^{15}$, calculate the energy of the source.
The longest wavelength doublet absorption transition is observed at 589 and 589.6 nm. Calculate the frequency of each transition and energy difference between two excited states.

The work function for caesium atom is 1.9 eV. Calculate (a) the threshold wavelength and (b) the threshold frequency of the radiation. If the caesium element is irradiated with a wavelength 500 nm, calculate the kinetic energy and the velocity of the ejected photoelectron.

Following results are observed when sodium metal is irradiated with different wavelengths. Calculate (a) threshold wavelength and, (b) Planck’s constant. Table: $\lambda$ (nm): 500, 450, 400; $v \times 10^{-5}$ (cm s$^{-1}$): 2.55, 4.35, 5.35.

The ejection of the photoelectron from the silver metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying the voltage of 0.35 V when the radiation 256.7 nm is used. Calculate the work function for silver metal.

If the photon of the wavelength 150 pm strikes an atom and one of its inner bound electrons is ejected out with a velocity of $1.5 \times 10^7$ m s$^{-1}$, calculate the energy with which it is bound to the nucleus.

Emission transitions in the Paschen series end at orbit $n = 3$ and start from orbit $n$ and can be represented as $\nu = 3.29 \times 10^{15} \text{ (Hz) } [1/3^2 - 1/n^2]$. Calculate the value of $n$ if the transition is observed at 1285 nm. Find the region of the spectrum.

Calculate the wavelength for the emission transition if it starts from the orbit having radius 1.3225 nm and ends at 211.6 pm. Name the series to which this transition belongs and the region of the spectrum.

Dual behaviour of matter proposed by de Broglie led to the discovery of electron microscope often used for the highly magnified images of biological molecules and other type of material. If the velocity of the electron in this microscope is $1.6 \times 10^6$ m s$^{-1}$, calculate de Broglie wavelength associated with this electron.

Similar to electron diffraction, neutron diffraction microscope is also used for the determination of the structure of molecules. If the wavelength used here is 800 pm, calculate the characteristic velocity associated with the neutron.
If the velocity of the electron in Bohr’s first orbit is $2.19 \times 10^6$ m s$^{-1}$, calculate the de Broglie wavelength associated with it.

The velocity associated with a proton moving in a potential difference of 1000 V is $4.37 \times 10^5$ m s$^{-1}$. If the hockey ball of mass 0.1 kg is moving with this velocity, calculate the wavelength associated with this velocity.

If the position of the electron is measured within an accuracy of $\pm 0.002$ nm, calculate the uncertainty in the momentum of the electron. Suppose the momentum of the electron is $h/4\pi_m \times 0.05$ nm, is there any problem in defining this value.

The quantum numbers of six electrons are given below. Arrange them in order of increasing energies. If any of these combination(s) has/have the same energy lists: 1. $n = 4, l = 2, m_l = -2, m_s = -1/2$; 2. $n = 3, l = 2, m_l = 1, m_s = +1/2$; 3. $n = 4, l = 1, m_l = 0, m_s = +1/2$; 4. $n = 3, l = 2, m_l = -2, m_s = -1/2$; 5. $n = 3, l = 1, m_l = -1, m_s = +1/2$; 6. $n = 4, l = 1, m_l = 0, m_s = +1/2$

The bromine atom possesses 35 electrons. It contains 6 electrons in $2p$ orbital, 6 electrons in $3p$ orbital and 5 electron in $4p$ orbital. Which of these electron experiences the lowest effective nuclear charge?
Among the following pairs of orbitals which orbital will experience the larger effective nuclear charge? (i) $2s$ and $3s$, (ii) $4d$ and $4f$, (iii) $3d$ and $3p$.
The unpaired electrons in Al and Si are present in $3p$ orbital. Which electrons will experience more effective nuclear charge from the nucleus?
Indicate the number of unpaired electrons in: (a) P, (b) Si, (c) Cr, (d) Fe and (e) Kr.
(a) How many subshells are associated with $n = 4$? (b) How many electrons will be present in the subshells having $m_s$ value of $-1/2$ for $n = 4$?
